name: semantic_v1
description: Honest semantic fixture for Rekoll retrieval quality: paraphrase positives, synonym/hypernym swaps, hard negatives, negative controls, over dev-memory-shaped synthetic content (fictitious company Nimbus Labs). Meaning != keywords by authorship; lexical overlap measured, never targeted.
license: MIT (original synthetic work, AI-authored: Claude Fable 5, Rekoll efficacy Lane 1b)

overlap.measure: max over gold docs of |T(q) & T(g)| / |T(q)|

hard_negatives.miner: okapi-bm25-stdlib (verify_semantic_v1.py; NOT the embedder under test, NOT SQLite FTS5)

adjudication.annotators: two independent AI passes (honestly labeled: AI, not human)

documents.text: Atlas incident 2025-11-03: the Redis session store hit maxmemory and started evicting live checkout sessions, so users were logged out mid-payment. Fix: dedicated Redis instance with noeviction and alerts at 80% memory.

Ember incident 2026-01-19: a cache stampede after a Redis flush took the support portal down for 40 minutes; thousands of concurrent misses hammered Postgres. Fix: request coalescing plus jittered TTLs.
Beacon incident 2025-08-27: a Redis cluster failover dropped the push-notification dedupe set, so users received duplicate pushes for two hours. Fix: persist dedupe keys with AOF and a replay guard.
Atlas decision: we chose PostgreSQL over DynamoDB for the ledger because double-entry bookkeeping needs multi-row ACID transactions; the audit table lives in the same database.
Cinder decision: feature values are stored as Parquet on S3 with DuckDB for ad-hoc analysis; we rejected a dedicated feature-store SaaS on cost grounds.
Ember decision: support tickets are indexed in Elasticsearch for full-text search while Postgres remains the source of truth; the indexer tails the WAL via Debezium.
Atlas constraint: the public payments API is rate limited to 100 requests per minute per merchant key using a token bucket in the gateway; 429 responses include a Retry-After header.
Beacon constraint: push notification sends are throttled to 500 per second per app to stay under the APNs and FCM quotas; overflow is queued, never dropped.
Ember constraint: the ticket search endpoint allows 30 queries per minute per agent seat; the limiter lives in the Node middleware, keyed by session id.

Atlas incident 2025-12-14: the TLS certificate for the merchant webhook sender expired, so signed webhook deliveries failed for six hours. Fix: cert-manager auto-renewal plus expiry alerts 21 days out.

Drift incident 2026-02-02: an expired internal CA certificate broke mTLS between services in the staging mesh; deploys were blocked all morning. Fix: rotate the CA yearly and alert 30 days before expiry.
Beacon incident 2025-09-30: the APNs push certificate lapsed and iOS notifications silently stopped for a day. Fix: switched to token-based APNs auth, which does not expire.
Cinder cost note: moving Spark shuffle data from gp3 EBS volumes to instance-store NVMe cut the nightly pipeline bill by 38%; spot instances now cover all non-critical jobs.
Drift cost note: right-sizing Kubernetes requests using VPA recommendations reduced the cluster node count from 60 to 41, saving about $11k per month.
Ember cost note: shrinking the Elasticsearch cluster to three data nodes and moving cold indices to searchable snapshots halved the search infrastructure spend.
Atlas decision: every payment mutation endpoint requires an idempotency key header; a retry with the same key returns the original response instead of charging the card twice.
Atlas how-to: to reissue a failed refund, requeue the refund intent from the dead-letter topic with the original charge id; never create a new refund object by hand.
Atlas constraint: ledger events must be published to Kafka partitioned by account id, so per-account ordering is guaranteed; cross-account ordering is explicitly not promised.
Atlas how-to: verify Stripe webhook signatures with the endpoint secret and reject anything older than five minutes to block replay; unverified events go to quarantine, never the ledger.
Atlas convention: all monetary amounts are stored as integer minor units (cents) with an ISO 4217 currency code; floating point money is banned in code review.
Atlas how-to: the nightly reconciliation job diffs Stripe payouts against ledger balances; discrepancies over one dollar page the payments on-call, smaller ones open a ticket.
Atlas constraint: raw card numbers never touch our servers; the frontend tokenizes with Stripe Elements, and PCI scope is limited to the token vault boundary.
Beacon decision: the mobile app queues user actions in a local SQLite outbox while offline and syncs with exponential backoff once connectivity returns; conflicts resolve last-write-wins per field.
Beacon how-to: register new deep link routes in the shared routes.yaml; both the Kotlin and Swift routers are generated from it at build time, so never hand-edit the native route tables.
Beacon constraint: marketing pushes must respect user-local quiet hours from 22:00 to 08:00; transactional notifications such as delivery updates are exempt.
Beacon decision: feature flags and A/B experiments run through our own flag service with deterministic bucketing by user id hash; we dropped Firebase Remote Config over stale-cache issues.
Beacon convention: a release is blocked if crash-free sessions fall below 99.5% in the first 24 hours of staged rollout; rollback is one click in the release dashboard.
Beacon how-to: profile photos are resized client-side to 512px and uploaded as WebP to the media bucket; originals are discarded to save storage and bandwidth.
Cinder how-to: to backfill a feature, run the Airflow dag with a bounded date range and the backfill pool, which caps parallelism at 4 so production tables are not starved.
Cinder convention: every feature table schema change goes through a versioned migration reviewed by two data engineers; breaking changes require a 30 day deprecation window.
Cinder constraint: features used by the fraud model must be fresher than 15 minutes; the pipeline emits a freshness metric per feature and alerts when the SLA is breached.
Cinder decision: raw event data containing personal information is dropped after 30 days; only aggregated or pseudonymized features are retained long term.
Cinder convention: Airflow tasks are written to be idempotent so retries are safe; any task that cannot be made idempotent must set retries to zero and page on failure.

Cinder incident 2025-10-08: the fraud model was trained on features computed with a different timezone than serving, causing offline/online skew and a spike in false declines. Fix: one shared feature computation library.
Drift how-to: production deploys go through ArgoCD sync from the release branch; direct kubectl apply against production is blocked by admission policy.
Drift decision: application secrets live in Vault and are injected at pod start via the agent sidecar; Kubernetes Secrets objects are used only for the Vault bootstrap token.
Drift how-to: to roll back a bad release, revert the release-branch commit and let ArgoCD converge; never delete pods by hand, the controller will fight you.
Drift convention: all cloud resources are managed by Terraform in the infra monorepo; console changes are reverted automatically by drift detection every hour.
Drift constraint: GPU node pools autoscale from zero and carry taints so only ML workloads schedule there; everything else runs on the general spot pool with a small on-demand floor.

Drift incident 2025-07-22: a wildcard DNS record deleted during cleanup took every preview environment offline for three hours. Fix: DNS zone changes require a second reviewer and a 24 hour deletion grace period.
Ember constraint: first response SLA is 4 business hours for standard tickets and 30 minutes for sev-1s from enterprise accounts; the SLA clock pauses while waiting on the customer.
Ember how-to: canned reply macros are versioned in the repo under support/macros and reviewed like code; editing them directly in the admin UI is disabled in production.
Ember decision: agent login uses Okta SAML single sign-on with a 12-hour session; password login is disabled except for two break-glass admin accounts stored in the vault.
Ember how-to: to improve ticket search, add domain synonyms to the Elasticsearch synonyms file (for example 'invoice' matching 'bill'); reindexing is not needed because synonyms apply at query time.
Ember convention: the CSAT survey is sent 24 hours after a ticket is solved, not immediately, because immediate surveys skewed scores negative; results feed the weekly quality review.
Ember constraint: ticket attachments are capped at 25 MB and scanned by ClamAV before agents can download them; executable file types are always blocked.
Atlas note: p99 latency for the charge endpoint is 340 ms, dominated by the external card network round trip; internal processing stays under 20 ms.
Atlas runbook: if Kafka consumer lag on the ledger topic exceeds 100k messages, scale the consumer group before investigating; the ledger writer is horizontally scalable.
Atlas convention: the payments on-call rotation is weekly, hands off on Mondays at 10:00, and always has a secondary in a different timezone.
Atlas how-to: the Stripe sandbox environment resets every Sunday night; seed merchant accounts are recreated by the fixtures job at 02:00 UTC.
Atlas note: chargeback disputes are handled in the Stripe dashboard by the finance team; engineering gets involved only when evidence upload automation fails.
Atlas note: the ledger table migration to partitioned storage completed in 2025 Q3; queries older than 18 months hit the archive partition and run slower.
Atlas note: VAT calculation for EU merchants is delegated to the tax microservice; Atlas stores the resulting tax lines and never computes rates itself.
Atlas note: outbound calls to the card network use a retry budget of 10%; when more than one call in ten is a retry, the circuit opens and sheds load.
Atlas note: the ledger runs on PostgreSQL 16 with synchronous replication to a standby in a second availability zone; failover is manual by design.
Atlas note: outgoing merchant webhooks retry with exponential backoff for up to 24 hours; after that, deliveries land in the merchant's dead-event feed for manual replay.
Beacon note: the iOS build takes about 14 minutes on CI, mostly Swift compilation; the module cache cuts warm builds to 5 minutes.
Beacon note: push delivery rate is tracked per platform; Android sits around 94% and iOS around 97%, the gap explained by background restrictions.
Beacon convention: App Store and Play Store releases are staggered by one day so a bad release never hits both platforms at once.
Beacon decision: in-app analytics events are batched and flushed every 30 seconds or 50 events, whichever comes first, to save battery.
Beacon how-to: new user-facing strings go in strings_en.xml and are sent automatically to the translation vendor; screenshots for context are attached by the l10n bot.
Beacon constraint: minimum supported OS versions are iOS 16 and Android 10; dropping a version requires a deprecation banner for 90 days first.
Beacon note: app icon badge counts are computed server-side and included in the push payload, because client-side counting drifted after force quits.
Beacon note: the local SQLite database is vacuumed once it exceeds 50 MB; heavy users were hitting 200 MB before vacuuming was added.
Cinder note: the pipeline runs Spark 3.5 on EMR; the upgrade from 3.3 required rewriting two UDFs that relied on deprecated APIs.
Cinder how-to: new datasets must be registered in the data catalog with an owner, a description, and a sensitivity tag before the first production run.
Cinder convention: every feature table carries data-quality checks for null rate and value ranges; a failing check quarantines the partition instead of publishing it.
Cinder convention: S3 paths follow s3://cinder-features/{table}/dt={date}/ with hive-style partitioning; ad-hoc buckets are forbidden.
Cinder note: exploration notebooks run on a shared JupyterHub with 32 GB memory limits; anything heavier should be a proper Spark job.
Cinder note: trained models are versioned in the MLflow registry; promotion to production requires an offline evaluation report attached to the version.
Cinder note: events arriving more than 48 hours late are written to a late-arrivals table and folded in by the weekly compaction job, not the hourly pipeline.
Cinder note: the recommendation embeddings are 128-dimensional and retrained weekly; nearest-neighbor lookups use an HNSW index in the serving layer.
Drift note: production Kubernetes is upgraded one minor version at a time, staging first, with a two-week soak before production.
Drift note: container images are stored in the internal registry with 90-day retention for untagged images; release images are kept forever.
Drift how-to: preview environments spin up per pull request under *.preview.nimbus.dev and are torn down when the PR merges or after 7 idle days.
Drift constraint: each team namespace has CPU and memory quotas; requests beyond quota need a capacity review, not just a bigger limit in the manifest.
Drift note: the service mesh sidecar adds roughly 2 ms p50 per hop; teams that need lower latency can request mesh exemption for specific routes.
Drift how-to: cluster state is backed up with Velero every six hours to the disaster-recovery bucket; restores are rehearsed quarterly in the DR game day.
Drift note: ArgoCD is pinned two releases behind latest after an upstream regression broke sync waves; the pin is revisited monthly.
Drift note: Vault audit logs stream to the SIEM; any root-token use pages security immediately, no exceptions.
Ember convention: ticket tags are lowercase kebab-case from a controlled vocabulary; free-form tags were removed after the taxonomy audit found 4,000 duplicates.
Ember note: Elasticsearch runs version 8.13 with security enabled; the upgrade from 7.x required reindexing all ticket indices over a weekend.
Ember note: the support engineering on-call covers the portal itself; agent workflow questions route to support operations, not engineering.
Ember note: the embeddable help widget is served from a separate CDN origin so a portal outage cannot take customer sites down with it.
Ember note: the portal UI ships in 9 languages; missing translation keys fall back to English and are reported to the l10n dashboard weekly.
Ember how-to: bulk ticket exports run as async jobs and deliver a signed download link by email; synchronous export was removed after it kept timing out.
Ember note: every agent action on a ticket is written to an append-only audit log retained for two years for compliance.
Ember note: the portal backend runs Node 20 LTS; the upgrade to 22 is blocked on a native dependency in the PDF renderer.
Nimbus note: Redis is approved for caching and ephemeral state only; using it as a primary datastore requires an architecture review exception.
Nimbus convention: every sev-1 incident gets a blameless postmortem within five working days, with action items tracked on the reliability board.
Nimbus constraint: all public endpoints must use TLS 1.2 or higher; TLS 1.0 and 1.1 were disabled fleet-wide in 2024.
Nimbus convention: the monthly cloud cost review happens on the first Tuesday; any service growing spend more than 15% month over month must present a plan.
Nimbus note: the shared rate-limiting library implements sliding window and token bucket strategies; teams should not roll their own limiter.
Nimbus convention: CI scans every commit for leaked secrets; a hit blocks the merge and auto-rotates the credential where rotation is supported.
Nimbus note: the approved datastore list is Postgres, Redis, Elasticsearch, S3, and DynamoDB; anything else needs a formal exception from architecture.
Nimbus note: cert-manager issues certificates from Let's Encrypt for public domains and from the internal CA for mesh traffic; manual certificate uploads are deprecated.
Nimbus convention: Kafka topics are provisioned through the self-service portal with mandatory retention and ownership metadata; ad-hoc topic creation is disabled.
Nimbus note: the platform team ships a shared idempotency middleware for HTTP handlers; adopting teams get replay protection without touching business logic.
Nimbus convention: no production deploys on Fridays after 14:00 or during the December change freeze, except approved security fixes.
Nimbus convention: heavy backfills and bulk exports must run between 01:00 and 06:00 UTC to keep daytime latency stable across shared databases.
